statusLine: EU fuel security remains under pressure with 19 of 27 countries critically short in at least one fuel type, while crude oil holds steady at $93.09 after a 28.4% surge since February.
fullAnalysis:
European fuel security shows persistent strain across multiple countries and fuel types in March 2026. While EU averages for petrol (96.3 days) and diesel (88.8 days) hover near the 90-day mandatory minimum, jet fuel reserves remain significantly below target at 87.3 days. The data reveals widespread critical shortages, with 19 of 27 EU countries falling below minimum thresholds in at least one fuel category.

The geographic distribution of shortages creates particular vulnerability. Major economies including France, Germany, Italy, and Spain face critical deficits in multiple fuel types. Luxembourg, Poland, Austria, and Romania show the most severe constraints, with critical status across all three fuel categories. Eastern European nations appear especially affected, with Poland holding just 22.5 days of jet fuel reserves.

Crude oil prices have stabilized at $93.09 per barrel following the dramatic 28.4% increase since late February's Middle East supply disruptions. This price level, combined with widespread reserve drawdowns, suggests continued market tension. The contrast between northern countries like Estonia and Latvia, which maintain substantial reserves, and southern/central European nations facing shortages, indicates uneven distribution of both supply security and strategic planning across the bloc.
keyPoints: 19 of 27 EU countries show critical shortages in at least one fuel type, with Luxembourg, Poland, Austria, and Romania critically short across all categories
Jet fuel reserves at 87.3 days EU-wide remain the most constrained, with 17 countries below the 90-day minimum
Major economies France, Germany, Italy, and Spain face multiple critical fuel shortages despite their strategic importance
Brent crude stabilized at $93.09/barrel after surging 28.4% since February's Middle East supply disruptions
Northern EU states like Estonia and Latvia maintain robust reserves while central and southern countries show persistent strain
